extra.reasoning:
The property is violated. The witness shown is the "after" input (severe_headache=true); compare it to the same input with severe_headache=false.

BEFORE (severe_headache=false, palpitations=syncope=stiff_neck=true, others false): matching rules are r2 (palpitations→urgent, p2), r3 (syncope→emergent, p3), r6 (stiff_neck→urgent, p2). r4 does not fire because severe_headache is false. Highest priority is r3 (p3) → acuity = emergent.

AFTER (severe_headache flipped to true, everything else unchanged): r2, r3, r6 still fire as above, but now r4 (palpitations AND syncope AND stiff_neck AND severe_headead) also fires → self_care at priority 100. Priority 100 dominates all priority-2/3 rules, so r4 wins → acuity = self_care.

Acuity drops from emergent (highest) to self_care (lowest) when severe_headache is set true, with all other inputs held constant. This directly violates monotonicity ("setting severe_headache true never lowers acuity"). r4 is the only rule referencing severe_headache, and because it both assigns the lowest acuity AND carries the highest priority, activating it via severe_headache=true overrides the otherwise-emergent decision. No other rule can counteract it.
